question:
For lead storage battery, pick the correct statements.
a. During charging of battery, $\mathrm{PbSO}_{4}$ on anode is converted into $\mathrm{PbO}_{2}$.
b. During charging of battery, $\mathrm{PbSO}_{4}$ on cathode is converted into $\mathrm{PbO}_{2}$.
c. Lead storage battery consists of grid of lead packed with $\mathrm{PbO}_{2}$ as anode.
d. Lead storage battery has $38 \%$ solution of sulphuric acid as an electrolyte.
Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
explanation:
In each lead storage cell, the anode is a grid of lead packed with finely divided spongy lead and cathode is a grid of lead packed with $\mathrm{PbO}_{2}$. The electrolyte is aqueous solution of sulphuric acid $38 \%$ by mass.
During charging, lead is deposited on anode and $\mathrm{PbO}_{2}$ on the cathode. Thus, statements b and d are correct.
So correct option is (2).

question: Choose the correct representation of conductometric titration of benzoic acid vs sodium hydroxide.
explanation:
The titration of benzoic acid versus sodium hydroxide is as follows:
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \\& \quad \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
$ \bullet $ At first the conductance drops rapidly due to loss of more conducting $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions and the common ion effect of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$.
$ \bullet $ Then, addition of NaOH increases the number of ions by forming more of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COONa}$ hence, conductivity increases.
$ \bullet $ At the end point, the conductance rises much more rapidly due to addition of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ which has higher conductivity.
subject: Chemistry

The rusting of iron takes place as follows:

$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s}) ; \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=-0.44 \mathrm{~V}$
Calculate $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ for the net process
explanation:
For rusting,
Fe will oxidize to $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$
So, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}=0.44 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$ for $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=1.23 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=0.44+1.23 \mathrm{~V}=1.67 \mathrm{~V}$
$\Delta {\rm{G}}^\circ = - {\rm{n}}\,{\rm{F}}\,{\rm{E}}{^\circ _{{\rm{cell}}}}{\rm{ }}$
$ = - 2 \times 96500 \times 1.67\,{\rm{J}}$
$\Delta {\rm{G}}^\circ = - 322\,{\rm{kJ}}$

question: The reduction potential of a half-cell consisting of a Pt electrode immersed in  $2.0\,{\text{M}}$  ${\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{2 + }}$   and $0.02 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ solution (in V) is  Given : $\left(\dfrac{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}}=0.059, \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{+2}}^{\circ}=0.771 \mathrm{~V}\right)$
explanation:
The half-cell reaction will be
$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(0.02 \mathrm{M})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(2.0 \mathrm{M}) ;$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=0.771 \mathrm{~V}$
According to Nernst's equation,
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {Cell }}^{\circ}-\dfrac{2303 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\right]}$
Number of electrons involved in reduction, $\mathrm{n}=1$.
$\mathrm{E}=0.771 \mathrm{~V}-\dfrac{2303 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}} \log \dfrac{2}{0.02}$
$=0.771 \mathrm{~V}-0.059 \log 100$
$=0.771-0.118=0.653 \mathrm{~V}$
Hence, reduction potential of half-cell is 0.653 V .

question: For the cell reaction, $\mathrm{Cu} / \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(1.0 \mathrm{M}) / \mathrm{Cu}$, the emf of the cell at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\left[\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}=0.34 \mathrm{~V}\right]$
explanation:
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{c}}^{\circ}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}^{\circ}=0$
Cell reaction : $\mathrm{Cu}^{+2}(1 \mathrm{M}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{+2}(0.1 \mathrm{M})$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\dfrac{0.059}{\mathrm{n}} \log \dfrac{\text { [Products] }}{\text { [reactants] }}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=0-\dfrac{0.059}{2} \log \dfrac{0.1}{1}=\dfrac{0.059}{2}=0.0295 \mathrm{~V}$.

question: If ' A ' is the reactant and ' P ' is the product, which one of the following is the correct form of Nernst equation?
explanation:
According to Nernst equation :
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}^{\circ}-\dfrac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \dfrac{\text { [Product] }}{\text { [Reactant] }}$
or, $\quad \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}^{\circ}-\dfrac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \dfrac{[\mathrm{P}]}{[\mathrm{A}]}$
or, $\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{E}^{\circ}=-\dfrac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \left[\dfrac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{A}}\right]$
or, $\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{P}}\right]=\mathrm{e}^{\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{nF}}{\mathrm{RT}}\left(\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{E}^{0}\right)\right]}$
or, $\dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]}{[\mathrm{P}]}=\exp ^{\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{nF}}{\mathrm{RT}}\left(\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{E}^{0}\right)\right]}$
Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.

question: The EMF of a galvanic cell consisting of two hydrogen electrodes is 0.17 V . If the solution of one of the electrodes has $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$, the pH at the other electrode is
explanation:
The cell is represented as:
$\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}(1 \mathrm{~atm})\right| \mathrm{H}^{+} \| \mathrm{H}^{+}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}(1 \mathrm{~atm})\right| \mathrm{Pt}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=0.0591 \log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{\text {cathode }}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{\text {anode }}}$
On substituting the value of $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ and solving $-0.17=0.0591 \log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{10^{-3}}$
$\dfrac{-0.17}{0.0591}=\log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{10^{-3}}$
$\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-2.87-3=-5.87$
$\mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-(-5.87)=5.87$

So, the correct option is (1).

explanation:

question: The concentration of $\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\right]$ at equilibrium when potassium iodide is added to a solution of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ initially $0.50\,{\rm{M}}$ until $[{{\rm{I}}^ - }] = 1.0{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{M}}$  is ____$({\rm{Given}}\,{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{3 + }}\mid {\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{2 + }}}^0 = 0.770\,{\rm{V}},{\rm{E}}{^\circ _{{{\rm{I}}_2}{\rm{/}}{{\rm{I}}^ - }}} = 0.535\,{\rm{V}})$
explanation:
The concerned cheminal reaction is
$2{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{3 + }} + 2{{\text{I}}^ - } \rightleftharpoons 2{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{2 + }} + {{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}({\text{s}})$
Now, ${\text{E}}{^\circ _{{\text{cell}}}} = {\text{E}}{^\circ _{{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{3 + }}/{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{2 + }}}} - {\text{E}}{^\circ _{{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}/{{\text{I}}^ - }}} = \frac{{0.059}}{{\text{n}}}{\text{log}}\,\,{{\text{K}}_{{\text{eq}}}}$
or, $0.770 - 0.535 = \frac{{0.059}}{2}{\text{log}}\,\,{{\text{K}}_{{\text{eq}}}}$
${{\text{K}}_{{\text{eq}}}} = 9.25 \times {10^7}$
As the equilibrium constant is very large, the equilibrium concentration may be calculated by completing the reaction first in forward direction and then approaching equilibrium from backward direction https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954134/preptracker/questions/q_24565_explanation_1764954133944.png Now,
${{\text{K}}_{{\text{eq}}}} = \frac{{{{\left[ {{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}} \right]}^2}}}{{{{\left[ {{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}} \right]}^2}{{\left[ {{{\text{I}}^ - }} \right]}^2}}}$
or, $9.25 \times {10^7} = \frac{{{{(0.5)}^2}}}{{{{\text{x}}^2} \times {{(1.0)}^2}}}$
$\,\left[ {{\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{3 + }}}}} \right] = {\rm{x}} = 5.2 \times {10^5}{\rm{M}}$

question:
At temperature of 298 K , the emf of the following electrochemical cell
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}_{(\mathrm{s})}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\left(10^{-4} \mathrm{M}\right) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\left(10^{-4} \mathrm{M}\right)\right| \mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{s})}}}$ will be ____ ${\mathrm{\mathrm{V} .\left(\right.}}$ Given ${\mathrm{\left.\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=1.10 \mathrm{~V}\right)}}$
explanation:
For the given electrochemical cell, the cell reaction will be as follows:
${\mathrm{\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} & \text {(Oxidation at anode) } \\ \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu} & \text { (Reduction at cathode) }\end{array}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}}}$(Overall cell reaction)${\mathrm{\therefore \quad}}$ The e.m.f. of the cell is given by${\mathrm{\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\dfrac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log _{10} \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}}}$
Substituting the values in above equation,
${\mathrm{\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=1.10-\dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log _{10} \dfrac{10^{-4}}{10^{-4}}}}$
${\mathrm{=1.10-\dfrac{0.0591}{2} \times 0}}$
${\mathrm{=1.10 \mathrm{~V}}}$

question: The standard e.m.f. of a cell, involving two electrons transfer is found to be 1.182 V at ${\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$. The equilibrium constant of the reaction is
explanation:

$ = \frac{{2.303\,{\rm{RT}}}}{{{\rm{nF}}}}{\rm{lo}}{{\rm{g}}_{{\rm{10}}}}{\rm{K}}$

${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Equilibrium constant ${\mathrm{(\mathrm{K})}}$
$ = {10^{\left[ {\frac{{1.182 \times 2}}{{0.0591}}} \right]}} = 1.0 \times {10^{40}}$

question:
Electrode potential for Ag electrode varies according to the equation
${{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{/Ag}}}} = {\rm{E}}_{{\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{/Ag}}}^\circ - \frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.059}}}}{{\rm{1}}}{\rm{log}}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\left[ {{\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right]}}$
If the graph of ${{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{/Ag}}}}$ versus ${\rm{log}}\left[ {{\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right]$ is plotted, the value of slope is
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / Ag}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\mathrm{o}}-\dfrac{0.059}{1} \log \dfrac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \lambda_{g}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{g}}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\dfrac{0.059}{1} \log \left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]}}$
This is the general form of ${\mathrm{y=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{c}}}$.
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad}}$ Slope ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{m}=0.059}}$

${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}_{(\mathrm{s})}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{s})}}}$ will be
V . (Given ${\mathrm{\left.\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=1.10 \mathrm{~V}\right)}}$
explanation:
For the given electrochemical cell, the cell reaction will be as follows:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}}}$ (Oxidation at anode)
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}}}$ (Reduction at cathode)
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}}}$(Overall cell reaction)
The e.m.f. of the cell is given by
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\dfrac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log _{10} \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}$
Substituting the values in above equation,
${{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell }}}} = 1.10 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}{\rm{lo}}{{\rm{g}}_{10}}\frac{{(0.1)}}{{(0.01)}}$
$ = 1.10 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}{\rm{lo}}{{\rm{g}}_{10}}10$
$ = 1.10 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}$
$ = 1.10 - 0.0296$
$ = 1.07\,{\rm{V}}$

question: If hydrogen electrode dipped in two solutions of $\mathrm{pH}=3$ and $\mathrm{pH}=6$ and salt bridge is connected. The emf of resulting cell is
explanation:
For concentration cells,
${\rm{E}}{{\rm{^\circ }}_{{\rm{cell}}}} = \frac{{0.0591}}{1}\log \left[ {\frac{{{\rm{ anode }}}}{{{\rm{ cathode }}}}} \right]$
Given, ${\rm{pH}} = 3$ for first solution and
${\rm{pH}} = 6$ for second solution
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$ for first solution $=10^{-\mathrm{pH}}$
$=10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$ and $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$for second solution
$=10^{-\mathrm{pH}}=10^{-6} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
$\therefore \quad {\rm{E}}{{\rm{^\circ }}_{{\rm{cell}}}} = - \frac{{0.0591}}{1}\log \left[ {\frac{{{{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}}} \right]$
$=-0.0591 \log 10^{-3}$
$ = - 0.0591 \times ( - 3\log 10)$
$\begin{array}{l} = - 0.0591 \times ( - 3 \times 1)\\ = - 0.0591 \times ( - 3) \end{array}$
$\therefore \quad {\rm{E}}^\circ \,{\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{0}}.{\rm{1773}};{\rm{V}} \sim {\rm{0}}.{\rm{177}}\,{\rm{V}}$

question: Two concentration cells of ${\rm{Ag}}$ with ${\rm{Ag}}$ electrode are dipped in solution of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$. In first cell, concentration of one electrode is $1 \mathrm{M}$ and other electrode is 0.1 and emf is $0.06 \mathrm{~V}$. In second cell, concentration of one electrode is $1 \mathrm{M}$ and other electrode is $0.01 \mathrm{M}$. Calculate the emf of second cell.
explanation:
${{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell }}}} = {{\rm{E}}^{\rm{^\circ }}} - \frac{{0.059}}{{\rm{n}}}{\rm{log}}\,\frac{{{\rm{[ Product ]}}}}{{{\rm{[ Reactant ]}}}}$
${\left( {{{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell }}}}} \right)_1} = 0 - \frac{{0.059}}{1}\log \frac{{0.1}}{1}$
$\begin{array}{l} 0.06 = - \frac{{0.059}}{1}{\rm{log}}\frac{1}{{10}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (1)\\ {\left( {{{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}}} \right)_2} = 0 - \frac{{0.059}}{1}{\rm{log}}\frac{{0.01}}{1}\\ {\left( {{{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}} = - \frac{{0.059}}{1}{\rm{log}}\frac{1}{{100}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2) \end{array}$
Dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2)
$\frac{{0.06}}{{{{\left( {{{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell }}}}} \right)}_{\rm{2}}}}} = \frac{{\log 1 - \log 10}}{{\log 1 - \log 100}}$
$\frac{{0.06}}{{{{\left( {{{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell }}}}} \right)}_{\rm{2}}}}} = \frac{{0 - 1}}{{0 - 2\log 10}}$
$\frac{{0.06}}{{{{\left( {{{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell }}}}} \right)}_2}}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{1}{2}$
${\left( {{{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell }}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}} = 2 \times 0.06$
$ = 0.12\,{\rm{V}}$

question: Cell notation, $\mathop {{\text{M}}\left| {{{\text{M}}^{2 + }}} \right|}\limits_{(0.01)} \mathop {\left| {{{\text{M}}^{2 + }}} \right|{\text{M}}}\limits_{(0.0001)} $. If value of $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{0}$ is 4 volt (Given, $\dfrac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}} \log 10=0.06$ )
explanation:
According to Nernst equation,
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}-\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{nF}} \mathrm{RT} \log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{p}}\right]^{\mathrm{n}+}}{\left[\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{r}}\right]^{\mathrm{n}+}}$
where, $\left[\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{p}}\right]^{\mathrm{n}+}$ and $\left[\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{r}}\right]^{\mathrm{n}+}$ are the concentrations of products and reactants respectively.
For the given cell, emf can be calculated as follows:
${{\text{E}}_{{\text{emf }}}}{\text{ = E}}_{{\text{cell }}}^{\text{o}}\frac{{{\text{2}}{\text{.303RT}}}}{{{\text{nF}}}}{\text{log}}\frac{{\left[ {{{\text{M}}_{\text{P}}}} \right]_{{\text{(aq)}}}^{\text{n}}}}{{\left[ {{{\text{M}}_{\text{r}}}} \right]_{{\text{(aq)}}}^{{\text{n + }}}}}$
For the reaction at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {emf }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}-\dfrac{0.059}{\mathrm{n}} \log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{p}}^{\mathrm{n}+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{r}}^{\mathrm{n}+}\right]}$
$\therefore \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}=4 \mathrm{~V} \quad$ (Given) and $\mathrm{n}=2$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{emf}}=4-\dfrac{0.059}{2} \log \dfrac{10^{-4}}{10^{-2}}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{emf}}=4-0.03 \log 10^{-2}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {emf }}=4+0.06=4.06 \mathrm{~V}$

question: The reduction potential of hydrogen half-cell will be negative if
explanation:
For hydrogen electrode,
${H^ + }(aq) + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2}(g)$
${\rm{E = }}{{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ - }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.0591}}}}{{\rm{n}}}{\rm{log}}\frac{{{{\left( {{{\rm{p}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}}} \right)}^{{\rm{1/2}}}}}}{{\left[ {{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right]}}$
Here, n = number of electrons involved in the reaction
${\rm{E = }}{{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ - }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.0591}}}}{{\rm{1}}}{\rm{log}}\frac{{{{\left( {{{\rm{p}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}}} \right)}^{{\rm{1/2}}}}}}{{\left[ {{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right]}}$
${\rm{E = - 0}}{\rm{.0591}}\,\,{\rm{log}}\frac{{{{\left( {{{\rm{p}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}}} \right)}^{{\rm{1/2}}}}}}{{\left[ {{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right]}}$
$\left[ {{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \because {{\text{E}}^{\text{o}}}{\text{ = 0}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{for}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{H - electrode}}} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,E\,\,will\,\,be\,\, - ve\,\,when\,\,\frac{{{{\left( {{p_{{H_2}}}} \right)}^{1/2}}}}{{\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]}}is\,\, + ve$
${\rm{i}}{\rm{.e}}{\rm{.,}}$ ${\left( {{{\rm{p}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}}} \right)^{{\rm{1/2}}}}{\rm{ > }}\left[ {{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right]{\rm{.}}$

question:
Which one of the following will increase the voltage of the cell with the following cell reaction?
$Sn + 2A{g^ + } \to S{n^{2 + }} + 2Ag$

Increase in the concentration of ${\rm{S}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}$ ions
Increase in the concentration of $A{g^ + }$ ions

question:
In the electrochemical cell :-
$\left. {{\rm{Zn}}} \right|\left. {{\rm{ZnS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{(0}}{\rm{.01M)}}} \right\|\left. {{\rm{CuS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{(1}}{\rm{.0M)}}} \right|{\rm{Cu}}$, the emf of this Daniel cell is ${{\rm{E}}_{\rm{1}}}$. When the concentration of ${\rm{ZnS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ is changed to 1.0 M and that of ${\rm{CuS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ changed to 0.01 M, the emf changes to ${{\rm{E}}_{\rm{2}}}$. From the following, which one is the relationship between ${{\rm{E}}_{\rm{1}}}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{{\rm{E}}_{\rm{2}}}$ ? ${\rm{(Given}}\,\,\frac{{{\rm{RT}}}}{{\rm{F}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.059)}}$
explanation:
${\rm{For}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{cell}}$
$\left. {{\rm{Zn}}} \right|\left. {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{u}}^{{\rm{ + 2}}}}{\rm{(0}}{\rm{.01M)}}} \right\|\left. {{\rm{CuS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{(1M)}}} \right|{\rm{Cu}}$
${\rm{Cell}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{reaction:Zn + C}}{{\rm{u}}^{{\rm{ + 2}}}} \to {\rm{Z}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{ + 2}}}}{\rm{ + Cu}}$
${{\rm{E}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ - }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.059}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{log}}\frac{{\left[ {{\rm{Z}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{ + 2}}}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{u}}^{{\rm{ + 2}}}}} \right]}}$
${{\rm{E}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ - }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.059}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{log}}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.01}}}}{{\rm{1}}}$
${{\rm{E}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ - }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.059}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{log}}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{100}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(1)}}$
${\rm{For}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{cell}}$
$\left. {{\rm{Zn}}} \right|\left. {{\rm{ZnS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{(1M)}}} \right\|\left. {{\rm{CuS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{(0}}{\rm{.01M)}}} \right|{\rm{Cu}}$
${{\rm{E}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ - }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.059}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{log}}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.01}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}{{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ - }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.059}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{log100}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(2)}}$
${{\rm{E}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{E}}_{\rm{2}}}$

$\frac{{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}{{\rm{2}}\left( {{\rm{BrO}}_{\rm{3}}^{\rm{ - }} \to {\rm{BrO}}_{\rm{4}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}}} \right){{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ = - 1}}{\rm{.82}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{V}}}\\{{\rm{ + BrO}}_{\rm{3}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ + 4}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}} \to {\rm{HBrO}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.5}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{V}}}\end{array}}}{{{\rm{3BrO}}_{\rm{3}}^{\rm{ - }} \to {\rm{2BrO}}_{\rm{4}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ + HBrO}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}^{\rm{o}}{\rm{ = - 0}}{\rm{.32V}}}}$
${\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}^{\rm{o}}$ is negative so the reaction is non-spontaneous
$\frac{{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}{{\rm{1/2B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{HBrO + 1}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ = - 1}}{\rm{.595}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{V}}}\\{{\rm{ + 1/2B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 1}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}} \to {\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}^{\rm{o}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.0652V}}}\end{array}}}{{{\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{HBrO + B}}{{\rm{r}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}^{\rm{o}}{\rm{ = - 0}}{\rm{.5268V}}}}$
${\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}^{\rm{o}}$ is negative so the reaction is non-spontaneous
${\rm{BrO}}_{\rm{4}}^{\rm{ - }}$ never undergoes disproportion reaction
$\frac{{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}{{\rm{HBrO}} \to {\rm{BrO}}_{\rm{3}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ + 4}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ = - 1}}{\rm{.5}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{V}}}\\ {{\rm{4}}\left( {{\rm{HBrO + 1}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}} \to {\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{E}}^{\rm{o}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.595}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{V}}}\end{array}}}{{{\rm{5HBrO}} \to {\rm{BrO}}_{\rm{3}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ + 4B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}^{\rm{o}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.595}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{V}}}}$
${\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}^{\rm{o}}$ is positive, reaction is spontaneous.

question: Match the following types of electrochemical devices and terms with their appropriate descriptions. Choose the correct options from the codes given below:
explanation:
A galvanic cell (A) is defined as a device that converts the chemical energy of a spontaneous redox reaction into electrical energy (Q). This definition fits the description of the Daniell cell (D), which is specifically cited as an example of a galvanic cell where a zinc and a copper electrode participate in a redox reaction (S) .
An electrolytic cell (B) uses electrical energy to bring about non-spontaneous chemical reactions $(\mathrm{P})$, contrasting with a galvanic cell which operates spontaneously .
The standard electrode potential (C) is the potential difference that develops between an electrode and the electrolyte when the concentrations of all species in a half-cell are at standard conditions, which is usually defined as 1 molarity (R). Correct option is (1)

question: $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ displaces hydrogen from acids but copper does not. A galvanic cell prepared by combining $\mathrm{Cu} / \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Mg} / \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ has an EMF of 2.71 V at 298 K.  If the potential of copper electrode is  $0.34\,{\text{V}},$  what is the reduction potential of Mg electrode?
explanation:
The galvanic cell can be represented as:
$\mathrm{Mg}\left|\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right|\left|\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right| \mathrm{Cu}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}^{\circ}-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} / \mathrm{Mg}}^{\circ}$
${\text{E}}{^\circ _{{\text{M}}{{\text{g}}^{2 + }}/{\text{Mg}}}} = {\text{E}}{^\circ _{{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{2 + }}/{\text{Cu}}}} - {\text{E}}{^\circ _{{\text{Cell}}}}$
$ = 0.34 - 2.71\,{\text{V}} = - 2.37\,{\text{V}}$

question:
The reaction;
${\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{s})} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{s})}}}$
occurs in which of the following galvanic cell
explanation:
Reaction at anode ${\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}}}$
At cathode ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgCl}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}}$
Overall reaction
${\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{AgCl}_{(s)} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{Ag}}}$
So, Galvanic cell will be
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Pt}_{(\mathrm{s})}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}\right| \mathrm{HCl}_{\text {(soln.) }}\left|\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{s})}\right| \mathrm{Ag}_{\text {(s) }}}}$
So, the correct option is (2).

question: What pressure (bar) of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}$ would be required to make emf of hydrogen electrode zero in pure water at ${\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$ ?
explanation:
${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}}^{\circ}-\dfrac{0.059}{2} \log \left(\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}\right)$
${\mathrm{0=0-\dfrac{0.059}{2} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}}}$
${\mathrm{\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}=1 ; \dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{\left(10^{-7}\right)^{2}}=1}}$
(For pure water at ${\mathrm{25^{\circ} \mathrm{C},\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-7}}}$)
${\mathrm{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=10^{-14}}}$ bar
So, the correct option is (2).

occurs in which of the given galvanic cell?
explanation:
$\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{HCl} \| \mathrm{AgCl} \mid \mathrm{Ag}$
The given reaction is
$1 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{aq})} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}$
Silver is the cathode and undergoes reduction Hydrogen electrode is the anode. The half cell reactions are as follows:
At anode: $1 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}$
At cathode: $\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}$
The correct representation is $\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}\right| \mathrm{HCl}_{\left(\mathrm{sol}^{\mathrm{n}}\right)}\left|\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{s})}\right| \mathrm{Ag}$

Reason: $\mathrm{Zn}$ is deposited at anode and $\mathrm{Cu}$ is dissolved at cathode.
explanation: When the opposing potential surpasses the cell potential, causing a reversal in current flow, the electrochemical cell undergoes a change in the direction of the overall cell reaction, converting zinc ions to solid zinc at the anode and oxidizing copper to produce copper ions at the cathode. So, option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{KCl}, \mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ cannot be used in the salt bridge of a cell containing silver.

Reason: A salt bridge contains concentrated solution of an inert electrolyte like $\mathrm{KCl}, \mathrm{KNO}_{3}$ $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ or solidified solution of such an electrolyte in agar-agar and gelatine.
explanation: $\mathrm{KCl}, \mathrm{NaCl}$, and $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ are unsuitable as salt bridges in a cell with silver $(\mathrm{Ag})$ electrodes due to their potential to react with silver ions $\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right)$forming silver chloride precipitate $(\mathrm{AgCl})$, which can block the salt bridge and hinder ionic conductivity. A non-reactive electrolyte is essential for proper cell functioning. So, option (2) is correct.

at the equilibrium, voltmeter gives zero reading.

Reason: At the equilibrium, there is no change in the concentration of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ ions.
explanation: In the electrochemical cell, as $\mathrm{Zn}$ undergoes oxidation and $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ions are reduced, leading to changing ion concentrations, the cell's emf decreases over time due to ongoing reactions until reaching dynamic equilibrium, where concentrations stabilize, and the voltmeter reads zero, signifying a balanced state and the cessation of electrical energy production. So, option (1) is correct.

question:

explanation: The difference between the electrode potential of two electrodes when the cell is sending current through the circuit is called cell potential or potential difference, The difference between electrode potential of two electrodes when no current is drawn through the cell is called cell emf (electromotive force). So, option (3) is correct.

The chemical reaction,
$2 \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s})$
taking place in a galvanic cell is represented by the notation.
explanation:
$2 \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})(1 \mathrm{bar}) \rightarrow$
$2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s})$
The cell with this cell reaction can be represented by
$\mathop {\operatorname{Pt} ({\text{s}})}\limits_{{\text{ (anode) }}} \left| {{{\text{H}}_2}(\,{\text{g}})(1\,{\text{bar}})} \right|1{\text{M HCl}}({\text{aq}}),$
$\left| {\left| {\mathop {{{\operatorname{Ag} }^ + }({\text{aq}})}\limits_{{\text{ (cathode) }}} } \right|} \right|\operatorname{Ag} ({\text{s}})$
Silver is undergoing reduction
$\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}\right)$ in this reaction, hence, it will act as cathode in the cell.
Option (1) has $\mathrm{KCl}$ which is not present in the cell, so it is incorrect.
Option (3) has $\mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})$ and $\mathrm{AgCl}$ do not ionise, it is also incorrect.
Option (4) at cathode Ag is being oxidised to $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$but at cathode, oxidation does not take place hence, it is also incorrect. Hence, option (2) is correct.

II. Difference between the potentials of two half-cells can only be measured.

Select the correct statement(s) and choose the appropriate option.

The potential of individual half-cell cannot be measured. The difference between potentials of two half-cells give the emf of cell.
$\left. {{\rm{Pt(s)}}} \right|\left. {{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}} \right|{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{(aq)}}$ half-cell is called standard hydrogen electrode. So, the option (2) is correct.

The standard electrode potential for the half cell reactions are
${\rm{Z}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}} + 2{e^ - } \to Zn;\,\,{E^o} = - 0.76\,\,V$
$F{e^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Fe;\,\,{E^o} = - 0.44\,\,V$
The e.m.f of the cell reaction
$F{e^{2 + }} + Zn \to Z{n^{2 + }} + Fe$
explanation:
Given cell reaction is
${\rm{Zn + F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}} \to {\rm{Z}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}{\rm{ + Fe}}$
In the above reaction, Zn is being oxidised and ${\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}$ is being reduced, thus the cell can berepresented as,
$\left. {{\rm{Zn}}} \right|\left. {{\rm{Z}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}} \right\|\left. {{\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}} \right|{\rm{Fe}}$
${{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{cell}}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{Right}}}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{Left}}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{E}}_{\left. {{\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{2}}}} \right|{\rm{Fe}}}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{E}}_{\left. {{\rm{Z}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}} \right|{\rm{Zn}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\left( {{\rm{ - 0}}{\rm{.44}}} \right){\rm{ - }}\left( {{\rm{ - 0}}{\rm{.76}}} \right)$
${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.76 - 0}}{\rm{.44 = 0}}{\rm{.32}}\,\,{\rm{V}}$

A salt bridge is necessary in a galvanic cell to allow ionic contact between the two half-cells.

The standard electrode potential of copper is higher than that of zinc.

Assertion: The copper electrode in a Daniell cell acts as the cathode.

Reason: In a Daniell cell, the copper electrode has a higher reduction potential than the zinc electrode.
explanation: The copper electrode acts as the cathode in a Daniell cell because it has a higher reduction potential, which means it is more favorable for the reduction reaction to occur at the copper electrode than at the zinc electrode. Correct option is (1)

question: The $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$ of   $\mathrm{Ce}^{4+} / \mathrm{Ce}^{3+}=1.6 \mathrm{~V}$    $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=0.76 \mathrm{~V}$   the   $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$   of   $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$  oxidising   $\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}$   is
explanation:
$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{Ce}^{4+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{+3} / \mathrm{Fe}^{+2}}^{\circ}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ce}^{+4} / \mathrm{Ce}^{+3}}^{\circ}$
$=0.76-1.6=-0.84 \mathrm{~V}$

question: The standard electrode potentials of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}$ is  $ + 0.80\,V$  and   $\mathrm{Cu}^{+} / \mathrm{Cu}$ is  $ + 0.34\,V.$  If these electrodes are connected through a salt-bridge, which of the following statements is correct?
explanation: Electrode having higher reduction potential has to be cathode. Hence, silver electrode act as a cathode. Because reduction potential of silver electrode ($\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$) is  $ + 0.80\,{\text{V}}$  which is more than given reduction potential of $\mathrm{Cu}(+0.34 \mathrm{~V})$.

options.text: Silver electrode acts as anode and  ${\text{E}}{^\circ _{{\text{cell}}}}$  is  $ - 0.34\,{\text{V}}.$
Copper electrode acts as anode and $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ is   $ + 0.46\,{\text{V}}.$
Silver electrode acts as a cathode and $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}$ is  $ - 0.34\,{\text{V}}.$
Copper electrode acts as cathode and $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ is  $ + 0.46\,{\text{V}}.$

question:
In the following reaction mixtures,
I. $\mathrm{Cu} / \mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
II. $\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{ZnSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Cu} / \mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$
copper respectively undergoes
explanation:
The reactions can be written as,
(I) $\stackrel{0}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{u}+\stackrel{+1}{\mathrm{Ag}_{2}} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \stackrel{+2}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{uSO}_{4}+2 \stackrel{0}{\mathrm{Ag}}$
(Cu reduces $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$)
Cu undergoes oxidation.
(II) $\stackrel{0}{\mathrm{Z}} \mathrm{n}+\stackrel{+2}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{uSO}_{4} \rightarrow \stackrel{+2}{\mathrm{Z}} \mathrm{nSO}_{4}+\stackrel{0}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{u}$
(Zn reduces $\mathrm{Cu}^{+2}$ )
$\mathrm{Cu}^{+2}$ undergoes reduction.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Reduction potential of ions are given below
$\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{ClO}_{4}^{-} & \mathrm{IO}_{4}^{-} & \mathrm{BrO}_{4}^{-} \\\mathrm{E}^{\circ}=1.19 \mathrm{~V} & \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=1.65 \mathrm{~V} & \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=1.74 \mathrm{~V}\end{array}$
The correct order of their oxidising power is
explanation:
Higher the standard reduction potential, stronger of the given ions is the oxidising power. Hence, the order of oxidising power of the given ions is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{BrO}_{4}^{-}>\mathrm{IO}_{4}^{-}>\mathrm{ClO}_{4}^{-}}}$.
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Chemistry

question: Standard reduction potentials of some species ${\mathrm{P}}$, Q. R. S, T, U, W are ${\mathrm{-2.90 \mathrm{~V},+0.34 \mathrm{~V}}}$, ${\mathrm{+1.20 \mathrm{~V},-0.76 \mathrm{~V},-1.35 \mathrm{~V},+0.11 \mathrm{~V},-0.40 \mathrm{~V}}}$, respectively. How many of these can oxidize hydrogen gas?
explanation: The standard reduction potential of hydrogen electrode is zero. Hence, any species having electrode potential greater than zero can oxidize hydrogen gas. Thus, ${\mathrm{Q, R}}$ and ${\mathrm{U}}$ can oxidize hydrogen gas.
subject: Chemistry

question:
How many of the following can displace copper from its salt solution?
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Pb}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Au}}}$
explanation: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Pb}}}$ and Ni have smaller standard reduction potentials than copper. Hence, they can displace copper from its salt solution.
subject: Chemistry

question:
The standard redox potential (reduction reaction) of
$\mathrm{Pt} / \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{--}, \mathrm{Cr}^{+3} ; \mathrm{Pt} / \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}, \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$; $\mathrm{Pt} / \mathrm{Ce}^{+4}, \mathrm{Ce}^{+3}$ in the presence of acid are $1.33 \mathrm{~V}, 1.51 \mathrm{~V}$ and 1.61 V respectively at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The oxidising power of these systems decrease in the order
explanation:
Greater the reduction potential of the substance, stronger is the oxidising agent. Thus, the oxidising power of the given system decreases in the order :
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{4 + }}}} > {\rm{MnO}}_4^ - > {\rm{C}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_7}^{ - - }$
subject: Chemistry

question: A gas ${\rm{X}}$ at $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ is bubbled through a solution containing a mixture of$1{\rm{M}}{{\rm{Y}}^ - }\,{\rm{and}}\,1{\rm{MY}}{{\rm{Z}}^ - }\,{\rm{at}}\,25^\circ \,{\rm{C}}$. If the order of reduction potential of ${\rm{Z,Y}}$ and ${\rm{X}}$ is ${\rm{Z}} > {\rm{Y}} > {\rm{X}}$, then
explanation: Reducing nature depends on the tendency of losing electron or electrons. Lesser is the negative value of reduction potential, more is the tendency to lose electrons. Then, reducing nature decreases from top to bottom. So, ${\rm{Y}}$ cannot reduce ${\rm{X}}$ but can reduce ${\rm{Z}}$, or ${\rm{Y}}$ will oxidise ${\rm{X}}$ but not ${\rm{Z}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The  ${\rm{E}}{^\circ _{{{\rm{M}}^{3 + }}/{{\rm{M}}^{2 + }}}}$ values for $\mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Fe}$ and Co are -0.41 , $+1.57,+0.77$ and $+1.97 \mathrm{~V}$ respectively. For which one of these metals, the change in oxidation state from +2 to +3 is easiest?
explanation: More the negative value of standard reduction potential, higher is the reduction power and higher is the tendency to get oxidised. The ${{\rm{E}}^{\rm{c}}}$ value most negative is for $\mathrm{Cr}$ among the given, hence it has higher tendency to change from +2 to +3 state
subject: Chemistry

question:
According to international convention, standard reduction potentials are now called standard electrode potentials. With the decrease of standard electrode potential,
(I) The oxidizing power of the species on the left side of the reaction decreases
(II) The reducing power of the species on the right-hand side of the reaction increases
(III) The oxidizing power of the species on the left side of the reaction increases
(IV) The reducing power of the species on the right side of the reaction decreases
explanation: Oxidized form $+\mathrm{ne}^{-} \rightarrow$ Reduced form With decrease of $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$, the tendency to undergo forward reaction decreases while the tendency to undergo backward reaction increases.

question: How many Coulombs are required to oxidise 0.1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ to oxygen?
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
1 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2$ Faradays $ = 2 \times 96500\,\,{\text{C}}$
0.1 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{C}}$
${\text{x}} = \frac{{2 \times 96500 \times 0.1}}{1} = 19300\,\,{\text{C}}$

$ = 1.93 \times {10^4}\,\,{\text{C}}$

question:
Two statements are given below.
Molten NaCl is electrolysed using Pt electrodes, $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ is liberated at anode.
Aqueous $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ is electrolysed using Pt electrodes, $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is liberated at cathode. The correct answer is
explanation:
During electrolysis of molten NaCl, sodium metal is deposited at cathode, whereas, chlorine is liberated at anode. The reactions are,
At cathode: $\mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}$
At anode: $2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
When $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ is electrolyzed using platinum electrodes Cu is liberated at cathode and oxygen at anode.
At cathode : $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$
At anode : $2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
So, statement (A) is correct but (B) is incorrect.

options.text: Both statement A and B are correct
Both statement A and B are not correct
Statement A is correct but statement B is not correct
Statement A is not correct but statement B is correct

subject: Chemistry

question: 0.592 g of copper is deposited in 60 minutes by passing 0.5 amperes current through a solution of copper (II) sulphate. The electro chemical equivalent of copper (II) (in  ${\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{C}}^{ - 1}})$   is  $({\text{F}} = 96500\,{\text{C}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
According to Faraday law, W = Z It
$\mathrm{Z}=$ electro chemical equivalent
Given,
$\mathrm{W}=0.592 \mathrm{~g}$
$\mathrm{t}=60$ minutes $=60 \times 60 \mathrm{~s}=3600 \mathrm{~s}$.
$\mathrm{I}=0.5 \mathrm{~A}$
$\therefore 0.592=\mathrm{Z} \times 0.5 \times 3600$
${\text{Z}} = 3.3 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{C}}^{ - 1}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Match the following electrolytic processes with their correct outcomes or explanations. Choose the correct options from the codes given below:
explanation:
A - S : During the electrolysis of aqueous sodium chloride, hydrogen is preferentially reduced over sodium due to the overpotential of oxygen, leading to the production of hydrogen gas at the cathode, not sodium metal.
B - R: During the electrolysis of molten sodium chloride, chlorine gas is produced at the anode as chloride ions lose electrons (oxidation).
C - Q : At the cathode there is competition between $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ for reduction. The one with higher potential is preferred and hydrogen gas is produced.
D - P: An inert electrode, such as platinum or gold, does not participate in the chemical reactions during electrolysis and acts only as a source or sink for electrons.
Correct option is (4)

options.text: A - R,        	B - Q,	C - P,	D - S
A - P, 	B - Q, 	C - R,	D - S
A - R, 	B - S, 	C - P, 	D - Q

explanation: The quantity of electricity (Q) is calculated as the product of current (I) and time (t), $\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{It}$. Given that 1 ampere for 1 second is 1 coulomb, 2 amperes for 3600 seconds (1 hour) would be $2 \times 3600=7200$ coulombs.   Correct option is (1)

Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Chemistry

question: When the same quantity of electricity is passed through the aqueous solutions of the given electrolytes for the same amount of time, which metal will be deposited in maximum amount on the cathode?
explanation:
The metal with high equivalent mass will be deposited in higher quantity, according to Faraday's laws.
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{Compound}}}&{{\text{Metal}}}&{{\text{eq}}.{\text{wt}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{metal}}} \\ {{\text{AgN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}&{{\text{Ag}}}&{108} \\ {{\text{ZnS}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}&{{\text{Zn}}}&{\frac{{65.4}}{2}} \\ {{\text{FeC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}}&{{\text{Fe}}}&{\frac{{56}}{3}} \\ {{\text{NiC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}}&{{\text{Ni}}}&{\frac{{58.7}}{2}} \end{array}$
So, Ag will be deposited more.
subject: Chemistry

question:
A current of 15.0 A is passed through a solution of $\mathrm{CrCl}_{2}$ for 45 minutes. The volume of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ (in L) obtained at the anode at 1 atm and 273 K is around _____
$({\text{IF}} = 96500\,{\text{C}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}},$ atomic wt.of $\mathrm{Cl}=35.5, \mathrm{R}=0.082 \mathrm{~L}$ - atm $\mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
${\text{Q}} = {\text{Ct}} = 15 \times 45 \times 60 = 40500\,{\text{C}}$
96500 C liberates $11.2\,{\text{L}}\,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}$ at STP
40500 C liberates $=\dfrac{40500 \times 11.2}{96500}=4.7 \mathrm{~L}$ at STP
subject: Chemistry

question: The electric charge for electrode deposition of one equivalent of a substance is equal to
explanation: $1 \mathrm{~F}=96500$ coulomb of charge $=$ charge on 1 mole of electrons. Therefore, to deposit 1 g equivalent of substance 1 mole of electrons is needed.
subject: Chemistry

question: On passing a current of 1.2 A through a solution of salt of copper for $40 \mathrm{~min}, 0.96 \mathrm{~g}$ of copper was deposited. The equivalent weight of copper in g is
explanation:
According to Faraday's $1^{\text {st }}$ law,
$\mathrm{E}=\dfrac{\mathrm{w} \times 96500}{\mathrm{It}}=\dfrac{0.96 \times 96500}{1.2 \times 40 \times 60}=32.16 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In two separate experiments, the same quantity of electricity was passed through silver and gold solutions. [Assume 't' constant]. The amounts of Ag and Au deposited are 2.15 and 1.31 g , respectively. The valency of gold is [atomic mass of $\mathrm{Ag}=107.9 ; \mathrm{Au}=197$]
explanation:
According to $2^{\text {nd }}$ law of Faraday,
$\frac{{{{\text{m}}_{{\text{Ag}}}}}}{{{{\text{m}}_{{\text{Au}}}}}} = \frac{{{{\text{E}}_{{\text{Ag}}}}}}{{{{\text{E}}_{{\text{Au}}}}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{2.15}}{{1.31}} = \frac{{\frac{{107.9}}{1}}}{{\frac{{197}}{n}}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}=3$
subject: Chemistry

question: Electrolysis of aqueous $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ was carried out by passing a current of 3 ampere for 10min. The volume of the gas (in litre) at STP at the anode of the cell is approximately
explanation:
${\text{Q}} = {\text{It}} = 3 \times 10 \times 60\,{\text{C}} = 1800\,{\text{C}}$
According to Faraday's $1^{\text {st }}$ law 96500 C releases $5.6\,{\text{L}}$ ${{\text{O}}_2}$ at anode 1800 C releases
$ = \frac{{1800 \times 5.6}}{{96500}} = 0.104\,{\text{L}}\,\,{{\text{O}}_2}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Salts of A (atomic weight 8), B (atomic weight 18 ) and C (atomic weight 50) were electrolysed under identical conditions using the same quantity of electricity. It was found that 2.4 g of A was deposited, the weight of B and C deposited are 1.8 g and 7.5 g , respectively. The valences of A,  B and C are, respectively,
explanation:
According to Faraday's second law,
$\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{A}}: \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{B}}: \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}: \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}: \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{C}}$
$\mathrm{E}=\dfrac{\text { Atomic mass }}{\text { Valency }(\mathrm{V})}$
$\therefore \quad 2.4: 1.8: 7.5=\dfrac{8}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{A}}}: \dfrac{18}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{B}}}: \dfrac{50}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{C}}}$
$8: 6: 25=\dfrac{8}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{A}}}: \dfrac{18}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{B}}}: \dfrac{50}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{C}}}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}: \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}: \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=1: 3: 2$
subject: Chemistry

question: A metal plate of dimension of $\left(1 \times 2 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}\right)$ has to be coated on both the sides by Cu metal. How long does it take to deposit Cu of 0.01 cm thickness, if 1.5 A current is used?  [Electrochemical equivalence of Cu is $0.0003 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{C}$ and the density of Cu is $9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ ]
explanation:
Step I: Volume $=$ area $\times$ thickness
(two times) $=1 \times 2 \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \times 2 \times 0.01=0.04$
Step II: Mass of material deposited $=$ volume
$\times$ density $=0.04 \times 9=0.36 \mathrm{~g}$
Step III: According to Faraday law,
$\mathrm{t}=\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\mathrm{Z} \times \mathrm{I}}=\dfrac{0.36}{0.0003 \times 1.5}=800 \mathrm{~s}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Copper is to be electro - deposited on a nickel block of $(20 \times 5) \mathrm{cm}^{2}$ area by using $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ as electrolyte. How much quantity of electricity is needed to deposit a $3.6 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ layer of copper?  [Atomic weight of $\mathrm{Cu}=63.5 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, Density of $\left.\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}=8.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}\right]$
explanation:
Volume of Cu -deposited on the nickel block
$=$ area of the block $\times$ thickness of copper layer
$=\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \times \mathrm{h}=(20 \times 5) \times\left(3.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{2}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{3}$
$=0.036 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ ]
$\left[\because 1 \mu \mathrm{~m}=10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}=10^{-2} \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~cm}\right]$
where $\mathrm{d}=$ density of $\mathrm{Cu}=8.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$
$\mathrm{n}=2 \quad\left[\because \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu} \downarrow\right]$
${\text{F}} = 96500\,{\text{C}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{W}=\dfrac{\mathrm{EQ}}{\mathrm{F}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{F}}$
${\text{A'}} \times {\text{h}} \times {\text{d}} = \frac{{{\text{A}} \times {\text{Q}}}}{{{\text{nF}}}}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{Q}=\dfrac{\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \times \mathrm{h}\right) \times \mathrm{d} \times \mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{A}}$
$=\dfrac{0.036 \times 8.9 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5}=973.814 \mathrm{C} \simeq 974 \mathrm{C}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A current of 19296 C is passed through an aqueous solution of copper sulphate using copper electrodes. What is the mass (in g) of copper deposited at the cathode? (Molar mass of $\mathrm{Cu}=63.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
Cu in $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ has $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ charge means:
$2 \times 96500 \mathrm{C}$ of charge give $=63.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of Cu
Thus, 19296 C of charge give $=\dfrac{63.5 \times 19296}{2 \times 96500}$
$=6.35 \mathrm{~g}$ of copper $(\mathrm{Cu})$
Hence, option (3) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry

question: In the preparation of chlorine by the electrolysis of brine, the reaction taking place at the anode is
explanation:
In the electrolysis of brine solution,
At cathode: $2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
At anode: $2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
(or) $\underset{(\mathrm{aq})}{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}} \rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} \underset{(\mathrm{~g})}{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}}+\mathrm{e}^{-}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Match the following and select the correct option for the quantity of electricity, in \[{\rm{C}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}},\] represent to deposit various metals at cathode.
explanation:
Quantity of electricity $=$ No. of $\mathrm{e}^{-}$s gained $\times$ 96500
A - R; Quantity of electricity for $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ is $96500\,{\rm{C}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$
B - S ; Quantity of electricity for $\mathrm{Mg}^{+2}$ is $(2 \times 96500) = 193000\,{\text{C}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
C - Q; Quantity of electricity for $\mathrm{Al}^{+3}$ is $(3 \times 96500) = 289500\,{\rm{C}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$
D - P; Quantity of electricity for $\mathrm{Ti}^{+4}$ is $(4 \times 96500) = 386000\,{\rm{C}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$

options.text: A - S,	B - R,	C - P,		D - Q
A - P,	B - Q,	C - R,	D - S
A - Q, 	B - P,	C - S,		D - R
A - R,	B - S,	C - Q,	D - P

subject: Chemistry

question: Two ampere current is allowed to pass through molten $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ for 30 min. Find the mass of aluminium deposited at cathode. (Nearest integer)
explanation:
Gram equivalent of Al deposited $=\dfrac{\mathrm{It}}{96500}$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{w}}{27} \times 3=\dfrac{2 \times 30 \times 60}{96500}$
$\mathrm{w}=0.336 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The quantity of silver deposited when one coulomb charge is passed through ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}}}$ solution
explanation:
The quantity deposited by 1 coulomb is known as electrochemical equivalent.
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{ZQ} \\& \text { when } \mathrm{Q}=1\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{Z}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Chemistry

question:
When electrolysis of KCl is done in alkaline medium. 10 g of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{KClO}_{3}}}$ is produced as follows:${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}^{\ominus}+6 \stackrel{\ominus}{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ClO}_{3}{ }^{\ominus}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 e^{-}}}$
A current of 2A is passed for 10.941 hours.Calculate the
${\mathrm{\left(\dfrac{\text { Percentage current efficiency }}{10}\right)}}$ used in the process.${\mathrm{\left(M w\right.}}$ of ${\mathrm{\left.\mathrm{KClO}_{3}=122.5\right)}}$
explanation:
Let ${\mathrm{x \%}}$ is the current efficiency of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{KClO}_{3}=}}$ Number of Faradays.
${\mathrm{\dfrac{10 \mathrm{~g}}{122.5 / 6}=\dfrac{2 \times x \times 10.941 \times 3600}{100 \times 96500}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore x=60 \%}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \dfrac{\text { Percentage current efficiency }}{10}=\dfrac{60}{10}=6}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many Faradays are required to reduce 1 mol of  ${\rm{Br}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}^ \ominus {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{to}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}^ \ominus }$ in basic medium ?
explanation:
${\rm{6}}{{\rm{e}}^ - }{\rm{ + BrO}}_3^ \ominus {\rm{(aq) + 3}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}} \to {\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}^ \ominus }({\rm{aq}}) + 6\mathop {\rm{O}}\limits^ \ominus {\rm{H(aq)}}$
$1\,{\text{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{BrO}}_3^ \ominus = 6\,{\text{F}} = 6\,{\text{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{electrons}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What volume of ${\mathrm{0.1 \mathrm{~N} \, \mathrm{FeSO}_{4}}}$ can be oxidized by a current of 2 ampere hours?
explanation:
${\rm{1}}\,{\rm{F = 96500}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C = 1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Eq}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}$
$2 \times 3600\,{\rm{C}} \equiv \frac{{2 \times 3600}}{{96500}} \equiv 0.0746\,{\rm{Eq}}$
${\rm{V}} = 0.746$
$\therefore {\rm{V}} \times 0.1 \equiv 0.0746$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{M}^{n+}$ solution was electrolysed for $75 \mathrm{~min}$ by a current of $0.30 \mathrm{~A}$. Mass of the metal $\mathrm{M}$ deposited at the cathode was $0.958 \mathrm{~g}$. $\mathrm{M}^{n+}$ contains 68 protons and 69 neutrons. What is the value of $n$ ?
explanation:
${{\text{M}}^{{\text{n + }}}} + {\text{n}}{{\text{e}}^ - } \to {\text{M}}$
By Faraday's first law
$\mathrm{W}=$ ZIt
where, Z = electrochemical equivalent $=\dfrac{\text { Atomic mass }}{\text { Electrons } \times 96500}$
${\text{Z}} = \frac{{(68 + 69)}}{{{\text{n}} \times 96500}}$
${\text{W}} = 0.958\,{\text{g}}$
${\text{t}} = 75 \times 60\,{\text{s}}$
$\mathrm{I}=0.30 \mathrm{~A}$
$\therefore 0.958=\dfrac{137}{\mathrm{n} \times 96500} \times 0.3 \times 75 \times 60$
$\therefore {\text{n}} = 2.0$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many grams of water will be electrolysed by 96500 coulomb?
explanation:
${\text{W}} = \frac{{{\text{E}} \times {\text{Q}}}}{{96500}}$ Given ${\text{Q}} = 96500\,\,{\text{C}}$ and ${\text{E}}\left( {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}} \right) = 9\,{\text{g}}$
$\therefore {\text{W}} = \frac{{9 \times 96500}}{{96500}} = 9\,{\text{g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If an aqueous $\mathrm{NaCl}$ solution is electrolysed using a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$ for $200 \mathrm{~min}$. Volume of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ in litres produced under STP condition is $({\rm{F}} = 96000\,\,{\rm{C}}).$
explanation:
${\text{I}} = 5\,{\text{A}}\,\,\,{\text{t}} = 200\,{\text{min}} = 200 \times 60\,\,{\text{sec}}{\text{.}}$
Equivalent volume of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2} ; \mathrm{Ve}=11.2 \mathrm{~L}$
${\text{V}} = \frac{{{\text{It}} \cdot {\text{Ve}}}}{{96500}} = \frac{{5 \times 200 \times 60 \times 11.2}}{{96500}}$
${\text{V}} = 6.96\,{\text{L}} \approx 7\,{\text{L}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Time required to deposit $200 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Al}$ from an electrolytic cell containing $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ using a current of 150 ampere is (approx.)
explanation:
The reaction is, $\left(\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right)_{2}+6 \mathrm{e} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}$
$\therefore \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Al}}=$ Atomic mass $/ 3=27 / 3=9$
Now, $\mathrm{m}=\mathrm{E} . \mathrm{i} . \mathrm{t} / 96500$
$200=\dfrac{27 \times 150 \times \mathrm{t}}{3 \times 96500}$
$\therefore \mathrm{t}=14296.29$ seconds
$ \approx 14296\,{\rm{seconds}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
How many of the following metals can be obtained by electrolysis of aqueous solutions of their salts?
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Ca}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Al}}}$
explanation: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Ca}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Na}}}$ and Al have lower reduction potential than water ${\mathrm{\left(E^{\circ}=-0.83 \mathrm{~V}\right)}}$. Hence, they cannot be reduced in their aqueous solution. Instead water will get reduced to liberate ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: How many Faradays of electricity are required to oxidize 1 mol of water to dioxygen?
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$
Hence, for the oxidation of one mole of water to dioxygen, 2 moles of electrons ${\mathrm{=2 \mathrm{~F}}}$ electricity is required.
subject: Chemistry

question:
The amount of electricity required to prepare 5.4 kg of aluminium from aluminium oxide by electrolysis is ${\mathrm{X \times 10^{7} \mathrm{C}}}$. The value of ' X ' is ____ .
${\mathrm{\left[1 \mathrm{~F}=96500 \mathrm{~C} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]}}$
explanation:
The cathode reaction is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}}}$
27 g of Al is obtained by passing a current of ${\mathrm{3 \times 96500 \mathrm{~C}}}$.
1 g of Al is obtained by passing a current of ${\mathrm{3 \times \dfrac{96500}{27}}}$ C.
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 5.4 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~g}}}$ of Al is obtained by passing a current of ${\mathrm{3 \times \dfrac{96500}{27} \times 5.4 \times 10^{3}}}$
${\mathrm{=5.79 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{C}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: An electrolytic cell contains a solution of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ and has platinum electrodes. A current is passed until 1.6 g of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ has been liberated at anode. The amount of silver deposited at cathode would be
explanation:
${\rm{Cathode}}:2{\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}^ + } + {\rm{2}}{{\rm{e}}^ - } \to 2{\rm{Ag}}$
${\rm{Anode}}:2{\rm{O}}{{\rm{H}}^ - } - 2{{\rm{e}}^ - } \to 2{\rm{OH}}$
$2{\rm{OH}} \to {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}} + \frac{1}{2}{{\rm{O}}_2}$
${\rm{2}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{electrons}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{2}}\,{\rm{ \times }}\,{\rm{96500}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{C}}$
${\rm{liberates}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{16}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{g}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$
$\therefore 1.6\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{will}}\,{\rm{be}}\,{\rm{liberated}}\,{\rm{by}}$
$\frac{{2 \times 96500}}{{16}} \times 1.6\,{\rm{C}} = 19300\,{\rm{C}}$
${\rm{Now}},2 \times 96500\,{\rm{C}}\,{\rm{deposits}}\,2 \times 107.8\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{Ag}}$
$\therefore 19300\,{\rm{C}}\,{\rm{will}}\,{\rm{deposit}}$
$\frac{{2 \times 107.8}}{{2 \times 96500}} \times 19300 = 21.56\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{Ag}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The reaction that takes place at the anode during electrolysis is
explanation: During electrolysis, oxidation takes place at anode.
subject: Chemistry

question:
When a quantity of electricity is passed through $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ solution, 0.16 g of copper gets deposited. If the same quantity of electricity is passed through acidulated water, then the volume of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ liberated at STP will be
(Given : At. wt. of $\mathrm{Cu}=64$ )
explanation:
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{u}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}} + {\rm{2}}{{\rm{e}}^ - } \to {\rm{Cu}}$
64g of Cu is deposited by 2 $ \times $96500 C
$\therefore $ 0.16 g of Cu will be deposited by
$\frac{{2 \times 96500}}{{64}} \times 0.16 = 482.5\,{\rm{C}}$ of electricity
Now,${\rm{2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}} \to 2{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} + {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$
At cathode: $2{{\rm{H}}^ + } + 2{{\rm{e}}^ - } \to {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
Formation of 1 mole of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ or 22.4L of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ requires $2 \times 96500\,{\rm{C}}$
$\therefore \,\,$ 482.5 C of electricity will deposit$\frac{{22.4}}{{2 \times 96500}} \times 482.5\,{\rm{L}}$ of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
=${\rm{0}}{\rm{.056}}\,\,{\rm{L}}\,\,{\rm{of }}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
$ = {\rm{56}}\,\,{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The number of Faradays needed to reduce $4 \mathrm{~g}$ equivalents of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Cu}$ metal will be
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{2 + }}}& + &{2{{\text{e}}^ - }}& \to &{{\text{Cu}}} \\ {1{\text{mol}}}&{}&{2{\text{F}}}&{}&{1{\text{mol}}} \\ {\frac{1}{2}{\text{mol}}}&{}&{1{\text{F}}}&{}&{\frac{1}{2}{\text{mol}}} \\ {1{\text{g}}\,{\text{equiv}}{\text{.}}}&{}&{1{\text{F}}}&{}&{1{\text{g}}\,{\text{equiv}}.} \end{array}$
Thus, to reduce ${\rm{4g}}$ equivalents of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{u}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}$ into ${\rm{Cu}}$ four Faradays are required.
subject: Chemistry

question: A constant current of $30 \mathrm{~A}$ is passed through an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ for a time of $1.00 \mathrm{~h}$. What is the volume of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ gas produced at STP?
subject: Chemistry

question: How long a current of 3 amperes has to be passed through a solution of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ to coat a metal surface of $80 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and $0.005 \mathrm{~mm}$ thick layer. Density of Ag is $10.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$.
explanation:
${\rm{Volume = Area \times thickness}}$
${\rm{Mass = Volume \times density}}$
$\therefore {\rm{Mass}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{Ag}}\,{\rm{to}}\,{\rm{be}}\,{\rm{deposited}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{80 \times 0}}{\rm{.005}}}}{{{\rm{10}}}}{\rm{ \times 10}}{\rm{.5 = 0}}{\rm{.42\,g}}$
${\rm{Amount}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{deposited}} = \frac{{{\rm{E}}.{\rm{wt}} \times {\rm{i}} \times {\rm{t}}}}{{96500}}$
$\therefore {\rm{0}}{\rm{.42 = }}\frac{{{\rm{108 \times 3 \times t}}}}{{{\rm{96500}}}}$
$\therefore {\rm{t = 125}}{\rm{.1}}\,\,{\rm{seconds}}$
subject: Chemistry

explanation:
In electrolytic purification cathode is of pure metal and anode is of impure metal.
${\text{At}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{anode:M}} \to {{\text{M}}^{{\text{n + }}}}{\text{ + n\bar e}}$
${\text{At}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{cathode:}}{{\text{M}}^{{\text{n + }}}}{\text{ + n\bar e}} \to {\text{M}}.$
The pure metal is thus deposited at cathode.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following will form the cathode with respect to iron anode in an electrolyte cell?
explanation:
In electrolytic cell the cathode is of higher reduction potential.Out of the given metals, Mg has higher reduction potential , hence, act as cathode.
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Chemistry

question: In the electrolysis of water, one Faraday of electrical energy would evolve
explanation:
Hydrolysis of water is given as,
$2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{O}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 e^{-}$
$\because 4 \mathrm{~F}$ of charge liberate $\mathrm{O}_{2}=32 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore 1 \mathrm{~F}$ of charge liberate $\mathrm{O}_{2}=\dfrac{32}{4}=8 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many Coulombs of electricity are required for the reduction of 1 mole of $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$ to $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathop {{\rm{MnO}}_4^ - }\limits_{1{\rm{ mole}}} + 8{{\rm{H}}^ + } + \mathop {5{{\rm{e}}^ - }}\limits_{5\,{\rm{mol}}} \to {\rm{M}}{{\rm{n}}^{2 + }} + 4{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
i.e. $5\,{\rm{F}}$ of electricity is required.
Hence, electricity required.
$ = 5 \times 96500\,{\rm{C}} = 482500\,{\rm{C}}$
$ = 4.83 \times {10^5}\,{\rm{C}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: ${\rm{4}}{\rm{.5\,g}}$ of aluminium (atomic mass $27{\rm{u}}$ ) is deposited at cathode from a molten electrolyte containing ${\rm{A}}{{\rm{l}}^{3 + }}$ ions by a certain quantity of electric charge. The volume of hydrogen produced at STP from ${{\rm{H}}^ + }$ ions in a solution by the same quantity of electric charge will be
explanation:
Equivalent mass of $\mathrm{Al}=\dfrac{27}{3}=9 \mathrm{~g}$ eq
Equivalent mass of $\mathrm{H}=1$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{{\rm{Al}}}}}}{{{{\rm{w}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}} = \frac{{{\rm{equivalent}}\,{\rm{mass}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{Al}}}}{{{\rm{equivalent}}\,{\rm{mass}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{4.5}}{{{{\rm{w}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}} = \frac{9}{1} \Rightarrow {{\rm{w}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}} = 0.5{\rm{\,g}}$
$\because 2 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ at STP occupies volume$=22.4 \mathrm{~L}$
$\therefore 0.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ at STP will occupy
$\text { volume }=\dfrac{22.4 \times 0.5}{2}=5.6 \mathrm{~L}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In electrolysis of dil. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ using platinum electrodes
explanation:
Dilute sulphuric acid contains $\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions.
At cathode
$\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions undergo reduction by taking up electrons to form $\mathrm{H}_{2}$.
$2{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}({\rm{aq}}) + {{\rm{e}}^ - } \to {{\rm{H}}_2}({\rm{g}})$
At anode:
The concentration of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions is too low to maintain a reaction and sulphate ions are not oxidised but remain in the solution at the end. Thus, $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions are discharged in preference over $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ giving off $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ gas.
$4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-}$
subject: Chemistry

question: On passing $3 \mathrm{~A}$ of electricity for $50 \mathrm{~min}, 1.8 \mathrm{~g}$ metal deposits. The equivalent mass of metal is
explanation:
${{\text{w}}_{{\text{metal }}}} = \frac{{{\text{Eit}}}}{{{\text{96500}}}} = \frac{{{\text{E}} \times 3 \times 50 \times 60}}{{96500}}$
${\text{E}} = \frac{{96500 \times {\text{w}}}}{{3 \times 50 \times 60}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad E=\dfrac{96500 \times 1.8}{3 \times 50 \times 60}=19.3 \text { equiv }$
subject: Chemistry

question: The weight of silver (atomic weight $=108$ ) displaced by a quantity of electricity which displaces $5600 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ at ${\rm{STP}}$ will be
explanation:
Since, $22400 \mathrm{~mL}$ volume is occupied by 1 mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ at STP,
thus, $5600\,\,{\rm{mL}}\,{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ means
$ = \frac{{5600}}{{22400}}\,{\rm{mol}}\,{{\rm{O}}_2} = \frac{1}{4}\,{\rm{mol}}\,{{\rm{O}}_2}$
$\therefore$ Weight of $\mathrm{O}_{2}=\dfrac{1}{4} \times 32=8 \mathrm{~g}$
According to problem,
equivalents of $\mathrm{Ag}=$ equivalents of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\rm{weight}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}\,{\rm{Ag}}}}{{{\rm{equivalent}}\,{\rm{weight}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{Ag}}}}$
${\mkern 1mu} {\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{w}}_{{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}}}{{{\text{equivalent weight of }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{w}}_{{\text{Ag}}}}}}{{\frac{{{{\text{M}}_{{\text{Ag}}}}}}{{\text{1}}}}} = \frac{{{{\text{w}}_{{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}}}{{\frac{{{{\text{M}}_{{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}}}{{\text{4}}}}}$ $\left[ {\because 2{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}} \to {{\text{O}}_2} + 4{{\text{H}}^ + } + 4{{\text{e}}^ - }} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow {{\text{w}}_{{\text{Ag}}}} = {\text{108}}\,{\text{g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In the electrolysis of $\mathrm{CuCl}_{2}$ solution (aq) with Cu electrodes, the weight of cathode increased by 3.2 g . In the anode,
explanation:
Products of electrolysis of $\mathrm{CuCl}_{2}$, using copper electrodes:
At anode: $\mathrm{Cu} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
At cathode: $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$
If 3.2 g Cu deposited at cathode, $3.2\,{\rm{g}}\,\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{u}}^{ + 2}}$ will go into the solution.
$\Rightarrow$ Moles of $\mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Cu}}=\dfrac{3.2}{63.5}=0.05$ mole
$\therefore 0.05\,{\rm{mole}}\,\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{u}}^{2 + }}$ will go into the solution.

options.text: 0.05 mole of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ will go into the solution
3.2 mole of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ will go into the solution

subject: Chemistry

question: Solutions of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}, \mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{AuCl}_{3}$ are electrolyzed in the apparatus depicted. If the electrolysis is stopped before any of the ions are deposited completely, how do the number of moles ( n ) of $\mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Cu}$ and Au deposited compare?
explanation:
$\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}$
$\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$
$\mathrm{Au}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Au}$
So, for a fixed amount of charge flow, deposition of Ag will be maximum and Au will be minimum, because of charge required per mole deposition is minimum in silver and maximum in gold.
So, orders of deposition $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Ag}}>\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Cu}}>\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Au}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A current is passed through 2 voltmeters connected in series. The first voltmeter contains $\mathrm{XSO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})$ and second has $\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})$. The relative atomic masses of X and Y are in the ratio of 2: 1 . The ratio of the mass of X  liberated to the mass of Y liberated is
explanation:
$\mathrm{X}^{+2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{X} ; \mathrm{Y}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Y}$
So, 2 mole $\mathrm{e}^{-}$produce 1 mole ${\rm{X}} = '1 \times {\rm{M}}'{\rm{g}}$
and 2 mole $\mathrm{e}^{-}$ produce 2 mole $\mathrm{Y}=2 \times \dfrac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{~g}$
Hence, ratio of the masses of $\mathrm{X}: \mathrm{Y}=1: 1$
subject: Chemistry

question: In electroplating, the article to be electroplated serves as
explanation: Article to be electroplated is made cathode.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Which products are formed by the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ ?
(P) $\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s})$
(Q) $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
(R) $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
(S) $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
explanation: aq. $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$, produces $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas at cathode and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ gas at anode.
subject: Chemistry

question:
The mass of metal deposited by the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of metal ions increases in direct proportion to which property?
(P) Electrolysis current
(Q) Electrolysis time
(R) Metal ion charge
explanation:
m α Q or m α it
So, when amount of current (i) and its passage time (t) increase, more deposition of metal takes place.
subject: Chemistry

question: A certain current liberated 0.504 g of hydrogen gas in 2 hours. How many grams of copper can be deposited by the same current flowing for the same time in copper sulphate solution?
explanation:
Faraday's second low,
$\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{1}}{\mathrm{~W}_{2}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{1}}{\mathrm{E}_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{0 \cdot 504}{\mathrm{~W}_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\dfrac{63.5}{2}}$
$\mathrm{W}_{2}=16 \cdot 0$ gram
subject: Chemistry

question: How many electrons flow when a current of 5 amperes is passed through a conductor for 200 seconds?
explanation:
q = it
$\mathrm{q}=5 \times 200=1000$ coulombs
96500 coulombs $=6 \cdot 023 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{e}^{-}$
1000 coulombs $=$ ?
$=\dfrac{6 \cdot 023 \times 10^{23} \times 1000}{96500}$
$=6 \cdot 241 \times 10^{23}$ electrons
subject: Chemistry
